coredata.dc:description: Quantum dots (QDs) are getting special attention due to their commendable optical properties and applications. Conventional metal-based QDs have toxicity and non-biodegradability issues, thus it becomes necessary to search for renewable precursor molecules for QDs synthesis. In recent years, biomass-based carbon rich QDs (CQDs) have been introduced which are mainly synthesised via carbonization (pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatment). These CQDs offered higher photostability, biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and easy tunability for physicochemical properties. Exceptional optical properties become a point of attraction for its multifaceted applications in various sectors like fabrication of electrodes and solar cells, conversion of solar energy to electricity, detection of pollutants, designing biosensors, etc. In recent years, a lot of work has been done in this field. This article will summarize these advancements along in a special context to biomass-based QDs and their applications in energy and the environment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Quantum dots (QDs) are getting special attention due to their commendable optical properties and applications. Conventional metal-based QDs have toxicity and non-biodegradability issues, thus it becomes necessary to search for renewable precursor molecules for QDs synthesis. In recent years, biomass-based carbon rich QDs (CQDs) have been introduced which are mainly synthesised via carbonization (pyrolysis and hydrothermal treatment). These CQDs offered higher photostability, biocompatibility, low-toxicity, and easy tunability for physicochemical properties. Exceptional optical properties become a point of attraction for its multifaceted applications in various sectors like fabrication of electrodes and solar cells, conversion of solar energy to electricity, detection of pollutants, designing biosensors, etc. In recent years, a lot of work has been done in this field. This article will summarize these advancements along in a special context to biomass-based QDs and their applications in energy and the environment.

ce:section.ce:para: The idea of ultra-small circuits and portable detection kits gave rise to a new area ‘Nano-technology’ that works for the fabrication of nano-sized structures like nano-fibres, nano-plates, nano-tubes, nano-wires, and nano-particles and made a significant contribution to the fabrication of biosensors (Hua et al., 2021; Prabha et al., 2021), energy (Tao et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021), and other healthcare equipment (Payal and Pandey, 2021; Sun et al., 2021). Quantum dots (QDs) are ultrasmall, nanostructures with physical dimensions smaller than the exciton Bohr radius. In layman terms, QDs are defined as nanostructures having at least one dimension below 10 nm and referred to as zero ‘0’ dimension. The QDs became reality with the fabrication of semiconductor nanocrystals in glass matrix in 1981 by Russian physicist Alexey I. Ekimov at the Vavilov State Optical Institute (Reed et al., 1986). The discovery was followed by the formulation of QDs in colloidal solution by an American chemist Louis E. Brus (Abdel-Salam et al., 2020; Hou, 2019). The contrasting and commendable physical, chemical and optical characteristics of QDs make them the centre of attraction and preferred candidate while fabricating several advanced instrumentations (Abdel-Salam et al., 2020; Rasal et al., 2021; Tang et al., 2021; L. Wang et al., 2020). Since the beginning, metals and semiconductors are the primary choice especially elements from II to VI or III to V groups and its applicability is more or less restricted to electronics but later the concept was also introduced to healthcare, textiles, and other industrial sectors. Moreover, heavy metals like cadmium used in QDs synthesis were started introduced into the environment and imposed toxicity on living systems and resulting in health complications including carcinogenesis, cells and tissue disruption, generation of reactive oxygen species and free radicals which interfere with normal physiological processes (Bellanger et al., 2015; Hu et al., 2016; Libralato et al., 2017; Wu and Tang, 2014).

Therefore, it becomes necessary to look for some renewable and less or non-toxic alternative for metal and semiconductors. Biomass is the most abundantly available raw material and is rich in carbon and various functional groups (Bhatia et al., 2020b). QDs can be synthesised from all kinds of natural materials such as biopolymers, saccharides, proteins, and lipids, and even waste biomass from agricultural and industrial sources including fruits and vegetable residues (Bas-Bellver et al., 2020; Brito et al., 2019; Ramírez-Pulido et al., 2021), garlic (Zhao et al., 2015), paddy straw (Kumar et al., 2021), wheat straw (J. Chen et al., 2021) and bagasse (Thambiraj and Ravi Shankaran, 2016), etc., irrespective of their composition and structure. However, their physical, chemical and functional properties rely on substrate composition and preparation method. The chemical tendency of carbon to bond with other elements allows the high possibility to tune in size, shape, and surface modifications as per need (X. Wang et al., 2019).

Several methods for QDs synthesis have been reported such as pyrolysis (Fig. 1 a) (Y. Xie et al., 2021), thermal and radiation abrasion (Fig. 1b) (Li et al., 2017), hydrothermal carbonization (Hasan et al., 2021), oxidation-reduction (Liu et al., 2016) and microwave and ultrasound-assisted approach (Fig. 1c) (Yan et al., 2021). The selection of method and reaction conditions depends upon the precursor used, required chemical, physical, and optical properties of QDs and applications (Kang et al., 2020). Due to the absence/restricted intramolecular quenching, QDs exhibit fluorescence of a wide range of intensity and colours depending upon the arrangement of atoms, type of hybridized carbon atoms, and possible deformities. The smaller the size, the lower will be the delocalization of electrons, and kinetic energy, and the higher will be the bandgap. Therefore, at lower size, particles usually emit bluish fluorescence of higher intensity while an increase in particle size and increase in acidic groups on the surface, the bandgap reduces and colour of fluorescence shifted towards red (Hesari and Ding, 2020; KhairolAnuar et al., 2021; L. Wang et al., 2020; Zhu et al., 2020).

QDs have applications in different sectors such as light-emitting devices (Vercelli, 2021), sensors (Kang et al., 2020), fluorescent labelling, bio-imaging (Zhang and Yu, 2016), and drug delivery (Ahuja et al., 2021; Nair et al., 2020). Gudimella et al., used Carica papaya leaves for QDs preparation and reported it for biomedical applications (antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activity) (Gudimella et al., 2022). Green-emissive carbon quantum dots were synthesised from pear juice using the solvothermal process and synthesised CQDs able to degrade methylene blue dye by 99.5% in 130 min (Das et al., 2019). In another study, the researcher reported that the attachment of antimicrobials (antibacterial and antifungal compounds) on xylitol based CDs reduced the dosage size against human microbial pathogens including Cryptococcus neoformans, Candida albicans, Streptococcus pyogenes (Ahuja et al., 2021).
Keeping in view the current advances in QDs synthesis and applications the present review is specially focussed on CQDs synthesis and application in the energy and environment sectors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantum dot synthesis from biomass using different methods
ce:section.ce:para: A range of biomass including agricultural and forestry residues, domestic waste, animal waste from the fishery, poultry, etc., have been reported for QDs preparation. However, QDs originated from organic wastes are usually CQDs due to the presence of carbon rich biopolymers like lignin, cellulose, and hemicellulose. In general, the synthesis approaches can be classified into two categories i.e., top-down and bottom-up approaches. In the former (top-down approach), the complex biomass is broken into its constituents by employing laser ablation, arc discharge, and electrochemical oxidation methods while in the later case (bottom-up approach), small sized particles are induced to combine and produce quantum dots. This method includes hydrothermal, microwave irradiation, and pyrolysis. Some of the major methods are discussed in detail below:

Laser ablation is a facile technique for the synthesis of nanomaterials. Laser ablation blazes high intensity laser pulses on a solid surface and transformed it to a plasma state due to created high pressure and temperature. Vaporized material condensed to the nanomaterial with high purity and exhibited a high rate of tunability for hydrophilicity, fluorescence, and particle size (Kang et al., 2020; X. Wang et al., 2019). Metal-graphite or metal composite are prepared by mixing or baking at high temperature before laser ablation and used as a precursor in laser ablation reactor under inert environment maintained by argon gas purging. Laser ablation was conducted by a laser beam of 532 nm which raised the temperature of the target surface to around 1200 ○C and vaporizes it. Target vapours took away from the reactor to the cooling zone by ‘Ar’ flow and deposited onto the copper collector, outside the furnace. Nanostructures obtained by laser ablation are of high purity which ranges around 90–95% but are usually contaminated with graphite particles and carbon dust (Szabó et al., 2010). Dried Platanus pomace was carbonized at 600 °C in a tube furnace filled under N2 environment. The carbonized sample was activated with alkali and again carbonized at 900 °C. The carbonized sample was dissolved in formamide, ethyl acetate, and ethylene glycol and undergone pulsed laser treatment to prepare modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) i.e.N-CQDs, CQDs-EA, and M-CQDs-EG respectively. N-CQDs exhibited indigo–blue photoluminescence emissions with a quantum yield (QY) of 32.4%. The fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was 6.56 ns. The intensity and stability of M-CQDs make it feasible for cellular imaging (X. Ren et al., 2019). In another effort, Wang et al. analysed the effect of physical and chemical processing environment on optical properties of soybean residues derived QDs prepared by hydrothermal carbonization. QDs prepared by hydrothermal carbonization of soybean powder at 200 °C for 20 h in presence of sulfuric acid. The prepared carbon emits blue photoluminescence. Thus, prepared QDs at 250–850 °C for annealing resulted in the loss of fluorescence however there was no effect on structure. QDs were mixed with NH4OH solution and undergone laser ablation and the processed QDs regained photoluminescence. Chemical analysis showed that laser ablation introduced ‘N’ as a dopant in QDs structure which is responsible for photoluminescence (S. Wang et al., 2020).

Arc discharge is another method like a laser treatment that can transform carbon substrate at the anodic electrode to gas plasma. The transformed plasma is recrystallized at the cathode to form nanomaterials. The whole process is conducted in a sealed reactor. An electric arc can raise the temperature of the reactor to 4,000 K that can create high-energy plasma. Arc discharge method was first introduced in 2004. To till date it is found that this method exhibited the high potential of tunability for quantum dots structure, luminescence properties, and intensity, solubility behaviour for solvents especially for water (X. Wang et al., 2019). Similar to laser ablation, the arc discharge method is suitable for carbon/graphite nano-structures and nano tubes. Graphite electrodes are the most common precursor used in arc discharge, but metal composites can also be used as the anode. Electrodes are kept at a minimal distance to allow the flow of the current of 50–150 An under low pressure of 30–130 torr and inert environment. At high temperatures, electrode material sublimes from the anode and form plasma. The stability of synthesised plasma can be increased by maintaining exposure time and current flow (Szabó et al., 2010). The product is also contaminated with graphite and carbon nanoparticles and dust. Both the above-described methods follow nearly similar mechanisms where precursors are transformed to vapour phase which further condensed to form nanostructures. Both shares limitation of product contaminated with carbon dust and unwanted nanoparticles. These methods are suitable for metals, graphite, and their composites. Both methods viz laser ablation and arc discharge are usually fit for graphite and metal precursors rather than biomass and waste as a precursor because of the heat transfer mechanism. Laser ablation transfer heat/energy on a focussed target point and in metals and other conducting medium heat can be diffused from the source point and distributed to other regionsas well. Biomass is a non conducting material in which only the target point received the energy while other points remain unaffected. It shows that heat/energy transfer is not efficient. In order to overcome the issue biomass is first carbonized with pyrolysis or other methods and then further processed by laser ablation in form solution.

Pyrolysis is also a kind of temperature-assisted carbonization that decomposes organic constituents at higher temperatures under an inert environment. It transforms the organic biomass into nanoparticles due to the cumulative action of dehydration, thermo-degradation, and carbonization with heat in an inert environment. Operation conditions like time, temperature, and heating rate have a direct influence on the structural and functional characteristics of products (Bhatia et al., 2021e; Kang et al., 2020). Based on the heating and operating temperature range, pyrolysis and thermal carbonization can be differentiated further. For both slow and fast pyrolysis, the operating conditions including maximum temperature range and holding time is more or less the same except heating rate i.e. in slow pyrolysis the heating rate is usually 1–30 °C/min while in fast pyrolysis is > 500 °C/min. The effect of operating conditions on the final product is clearly visible as slow pyrolysis generates charcoal while in fast pyrolysis the final product is usually oil and syngas (Abbas et al., 2018). Processing temperature affects QDs yield and physical and functional properties of QDs. The CQDs prepared at a higher temperature range tend to have a neutral surface and are mainly composed of carbon backbone while in a lower temperature range the QDs may have additional elements and functional groups. In addition, the particle size also reduces with an increase in carbonization temperature along with the destruction of optical properties (Kang et al., 2020). Bagheri et al. studied the effect of pyrolysis temperature (160–220 °C) on citric acids and reported twice quantum yield at a lower temperature (160 °C) as compared to high temperature have reported a reduction in QY in citric acid carbon dots when carbonization temperature increased from 160 to 220 °C (Bagheri et al., 2017).

A similar kind of variation was observed in temperature-assisted carbonization. Usually, thermal decomposition is performed in presence of water to improve the results. The variation in the thermal decomposition profile is mainly associated with the maximum temperature range. Low-temperature hydrothermal carbonization operates at 200–300 °C and yielded carbonaceous byproducts while high-temperature carbonization (400–800 °C) yields activated carbon, carbon rich nano-materials, and graphite (Abbas et al., 2018). Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were prepared from turbostratic carbon generated from waste neem leaves at 1000 °C at 5 h in a split-tube furnace which was then refluxed with an acidic mixture of sulfuric acid and nitric acid (3:1) at 90 °C for 5 h for GQDs preparation. GQDs thus prepared as brown powder were subjected to surface modification (amine functionalization) with ammonia solution via hydrothermal treatment. It was found that amine functionalization improved the dispersion of GQDs due to repulsion between amine groups which also affect the luminescence properties and QY of QDs. In comparison to GQDs, the intensity and QY of Am-GQDs increased and showed applications for the detection of l-cysteine and Ag+ (Suryawanshi et al., 2014). Ren and colleagues prepared carbon QDs from biomass under varying pyrolysis temperatures i.e., 300, 350, 400, 500 °C under nitrogen atmosphere. All the QDs prepared at different pyrolysis temperatures have commendable fluorescence however their structure and luminescence behaviour changes with an increase in temperature. On increase in temperature, disordered structure and heteroatom contamination increased. Moreover, the fluorescence against visible and UV light (365 nm) intensity and colour become darker with temperature (R. Ren et al., 2019).

Pyrolysis is one of the simplest and conventional methods that can be operated easily in the absence of air/oxygen or with limited and selective purging. The major issue with this system is high cost and low heat transfer along with variation in temperature as the quantity of heat reduced as moved from proximal to the distal end in a sample from heat source. Thus, it becomes necessary to find other modes for heat transfer with improved rate and low operational cost and this need led to the origin of microwave and ultrasonication assisted carbonization.

In recent times, microwave treatment has been adopted widely for the thermal synthesis of QDs and even for pyrolysis due to simple operation, higher efficiency, and cost-effectiveness (Kang et al., 2020). The basic principle of microwave assisted carbonization is similar to thermal carbonization as heat is the main factor that assists. QDs synthesis however there is a significant difference in the mode of heat transfer between conventional and microwave assisted carbonization. MwC uses electro-magnetic waves of frequency ranging from 300 MHz to 300 GHz. In contrast to the conventional mode of carbonization, Mw interacts with the electric dipole of molecules. The molecular dipole and water molecule dipole align themselves to the microwave electric field. In the process, heat is generated and transferred within a sample. The heat transfer is uniform and homogenous with minimal energy loss while in conventional mode, heating is not uniform and reduced at the distal end from the source. In addition, heating and carbonization are much rapid in comparison to the conventional approach where heat is transferred via convection. The above discussed features make microwave assisted carbonization more efficient, economic, and eco-friendly (Guiotoku et al., 2011). Guiotoku and colleagues prepared hydrochar from cellulose by Mw-assisted hydrothermal carbonization. The research group confirmed that produced hydrochar was rich in carbon. The precursor is transformed to microsphere aggregate composed of both aliphatic and aromatic structures and carbonyl functional groups (Guiotoku et al., 2012).

Microwave treatment also supports one pot synthesis approach for QDs synthesis which reduces the time and associated cost. Wang et al. synthesised CDs from protein-rich eggshell membranes ashes by microwave-assisted carbonization (Wang et al., 2012). Eggshell membrane was combusted at 400 °C for 2 h in a muffle furnace. The ash was dissolved in NaOH and undergone microwave treatment for 5 min. The prepared CDs exhibited maximum emission at 450 nm and have a QY of 14%. GQDs prepared from spent tea waste by pyrolysis at 500 °C for 3 h in a VCTF4 furnace. Obtained carbon ash was dissolved in distilled water and nitric acid followed by microwave treatment at 100–900 W for 10–15 min. Microwave treatment and time of operation increased to assess its effect on luminescence of GQDs. QDs prepared at 500 W for 120 min exhibited maximum luminescence which lowered at both the extreme ends (wattage and time) (Abbas et al., 2020). Integration of MwC coupled with plasma decomposition reduced the time required for CQDs preparation from fenugreek seeds. In comparison to conventional thermal carbonization, the speed increased by more than 97% (Dager et al., 2020). MwC may be performed with or without using passivation agents like ethylene diamine (EDA). Xylitol alcohol solution (XCDs) carbonized in presence of EDA in 700 W household microwave. EDA successfully protected the functional moieties even after microwave treatment under an acidic environment. Prepared XCDs were of 8 nm while the majority of dots belong to 3–4 nm range. Due to the diverse particle size range, the fluorescence emission is also scattered over a wide range (Ahuja et al., 2021).

Ultrasonic treatment is an easy and low-cost method for large scale synthesis of QDs from waste. It is suitable for hybrid QDs as it can accommodate both the main carbon source and solvents. In brief, it is a simpler method with a high potential of tunability with respect to experimental conditions (Kang et al., 2020). The exact mechanism is still unknown but it is hypothesised that cavitation may be responsible for QDs formation and crucial for controlling their characteristics. Ultrasound waves create acoustic cavitation and a transient environment with typically high temperature and pressure usually in the range of 5000 K and 1000 atm respectively. This transient extreme environment induces the change in the physical and chemical structure of the sample solution. Sonication amplified the degree of carbonization but maintain the sp2 carbon backbone which is critical for higher photostability. Sonication based approach allows the synthesis of QDs with variable structural and chemical properties by adjusting reaction parameters like sonication frequency, time, and precursors (Kumar et al., 2020). It has been seen that acoustic cavitation is one of the most critical factors for QDs synthesis however, the phenomena is greatly influenced by the type of solution, presence of a solvent, surfactants, etc. With respect to solvent and solution, boiling point, vapour pressure, viscosity, and surface tension changes which affect the mass transfer, and energy transfer. The addition of surfactants and solvents reduces the surface tension and increases the stability of nanostructures. Besides medium, sonication frequency, power, and duration directly affect the shape, size, and structure of nanoparticles. Higher frequency, and power reduce the size of bubble and cavitation and thus the size of particles, while at a lower frequency and power the size of particles increases. Previous researchhave proposed the optimum frequency of 100–400 kHz and power of 20–30 W for nanoparticle synthesis. Longer sonication generates heterogeneous particle size as it induces the deposition of more particles on the surface of pre-synthesised particles and thus increased the particle size. In comparison to spherical QDs, large sized nano-structures need a longer duration of sonication (Kumar et al., 2020). Carbon quantum dots were synthesised from sugarcane bagasse pulp by ultrasonication. The pulp was combusted at 60 °C in an open atmosphere to recover carbon which was dispersed in toluene for 24 h followed by ultrasonication for 1 h. Black precipitate was removed by centrifugation and CQDs were recovered in supernatant liquid. The average size of prepared QDs was 4.1 ± 0.17 nm that exhibits fluorescence. The QY of CQDs at 283 nm adsorption wavelength was 18.3%. The high intensity of fluorescence allows its use in bio-imaging (Thambiraj and Ravi Shankaran, 2016). Boruah and colleagues synthesised CQDs from garlic peels (GP), sugarcane bagasse (SCB), and taro peels (TP). The biomass was carbonized and undergone wet chemical oxidation in presence of H2O2. The CQDs were highly soluble in water and exhibited strong blue-fluorescence under UV light with 4–27% QY (Boruah et al., 2020).

Thermal methods need to expose precursors to high temperature and pressure, therefore, required specialized instruments and a durable setup. QDs preparation at milder conditions needs to develop some operating procedures which can be operated with only conventional setups. In the search for convenient and operable methods, the electrochemical oxidation method is one of them. The method relies on normal chemical reactions like oxidation and reduction.

ECO is a versatile method for QDs synthesis from a wide range of precursors. Carbon based electrodes immersed in an aqueous electrolyte. Graphite rods, prepared by densely packed carbon are used as an electrode for ECO. During electrode preparation, some defective sites are created in electrodes which became reaction centers for ECO. H+ and OH− ions and free radicals are created from the aqueous electrolyte in addition to electrolyte ions. These free radicals and ions generated from water electrolysis upon applying redox potential act as electrochemical scissors. In moieties entered in graphite layers and occupy van der Waal gaps from the defected sites and initiate redox reactions followed by cleavage of C–C to synthesis GQDs and GOQDs (Ahirwar et al., 2017). ECO is a simpler and low-cost operation, and easy to scaleup with minimal use of hazardous chemicals. On the other hand, size control and separation of QDs are still tedious tasks for traditional electrochemical synthesis. Liu et al. synthesised CQDs in three electrode system (graphite/platinum/silver) in alkali-ethanol (ethanol: alcohol: water-35mL: 8 mL: 0.3 g) for Fe+3 detection. For synthesis, oxidation was performed at three voltages (3, 5, 7 V). It was reported that QY of QDs was maximum (11.2%) in 5 V in comparison to 9.5% and 4.6% for 3 and 7 V respectively. Precise fluorescence quenching response against Fe+3 allowed its detection from tap water that can be used for probe designing for metal detection (Liu et al., 2016).

N doped GQDs were prepared using graphite electrodes by ECO. Both electrodes were kept in NaOH-semicarbazide electrolyte at 1 cm apart under 5 V working voltage. The process was performed for up to 4 h during which colourless electrolyte changes its colour to yellow and finally dark brown. The prepared GQDs were N doped and used for the detection of metal ions. As discussed above, the applied potential of 5 V, higher than the electrochemical potential of water viz 1.23 V which leads to electrolysis of water and generates hydroxyl and oxygen radicals. Generated radicals promote intercalation in graphite layers and thus synthesised nanostructures contain carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl functional groups. Semicarbazide, present in electrolyte exhibited condensation reaction with carbonyl group and functionalized the GQDs (Fu et al., 2019).

In comparison to others methods, solvothermal method has gained more popularity as it allows the synthesis of QDs from hybrid precursors. Precursor molecules are dissolved in solvents and kept in a reactor or autoclave with a Teflon lined reactor for thermal treatment in an inert environment or in air. Thermal treatment converts solubilized precursor to QDs. User and eco-friendly operation, low-cost, and no need of specialized equipment are some other advantages offered by the solvothermal approach (Kang et al., 2020). It is a simple and cost-effective method, widely preferred for the synthesis of QDs from a wide range of substrates like saccharides and biopolymers without extensive use of toxic chemicals and power which makes it a green and eco-friendly approach. In the case of biomass, water is the most common solvent used and therefore termed as hydrothermal carbonization. The aqueous solution of precursor molecule/s is kept in the hydrothermal reactor at high temperature. For hydrothermal carbonization, incubation temperature and operational time are the two most critical parameters that govern both structure and optical behaviour of QDs (Alaghmandfard et al., 2021). Hydrothermal treatment hydrolyses polymeric structure; break bonds and transform it into monomers. For example, during hydrothermal synthesis from biomass components which is mainly composed of cellulose, hemicellulose, chitin, and lignin, polymeric structure hydrolysed and disintegrated to monomeric sugars and aromatic alcohols. Small sized molecules and monomers, thus formed undergo decomposition, dehydration, and isomerisation at higher temperatures to form aldehydes, furfurals, organic acids, and ketones. Under high temperature and pressure, hydronium ions generated by acids act as a catalyst to induce condensation, dehydration, polymerization, and condensation of small molecules to form polymeric products. Aromatization is another aspect of hydrothermal treatment is the formation of aromatic clusters via cycloaddition reactions and aldol condensation followed by nucleation when aromatic clusters reach to critical supersaturation point and finally form QDs and other nano-structure (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021). Hydrothermal synthesis of QDs is a greener approach but the uncontrolled size is the major drawback for hydrothermal carbonization (Alaghmandfard et al., 2021).

Several researchers have studied QDs synthesis from various biomass and residual materials. Yao et al. synthesised hybrid QDs (CQDs/BiOI) with glucose and bismuth nitrate, dissolved in potassium iodide solution. Pre-processed CQDs were mixed with bismuth nitrate and undergone hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 2 h. Hybrid CQDs/BiOI were microflower shaped and have a higher potential of photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue (MB) under visible light (Yao et al., 2018). Dried and powdered residues of Citrofortunellamacrocarpapeels and Ananas comosus crowns were mixed with acid and undergone hydrothermal treatment at 180 and 220 °C in the presence and absence of tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA). The effect of acid type, temperature, and presence TEPA on optical properties of QDs was investigated. QDs prepared in sulfuric acid and nitric acid exhibited green and blue luminescence respectively. The addition of TEPA reduced the intensity of fluorescence and add TEPA moiety to carbon (de Yro et al., 2019). Xu et al. prepared CQDs emitting green fluorescence from bran and tartaric acid as dual carbon sources by one-pot solvothermal treatment for selective detection of Cu+2. Bran and tartaric acid were dissolved in dimethylformamide and kept at 150 °C for 8 h. Hybrid QDs was of 4.85 nm average size and rich in –OH, C–N, N–H, and –COOH functional groups. QDs exhibited maximum emission at 539 nm with respect to 450 nm excitation wavelength and have QY of 46%. The high intensity of QDs helped in designing fluorescent probes for Cu2+ detection. The probe can detect Cu2+in threshold limit of 0.0507 μM and is highly stable besides excellent luminescence (Xu et al., 2020). Hasan and colleagues prepared QDs from commercial-grade hydroxymethylfurfural, furfural, and microcrystalline cellulose via hydrothermal carbonization at 220 °C for 30 min. QDs prepared from three different raw materials exhibited significant variation in structural, functional, and optical behaviour. QDs prepared from cellulose have only CO functional group and have maximum emission intensity while furfural and HMF have a wide range of functional groups but have lower absorption and emission intensity than cellulose (Hasan et al., 2021).

It is one of the advanced methods for QDs synthesis, first validated by Gu et al., in 2017. He synthesised HKUST-1, ZIF-8, MIL-101 QDs of ∼1.35 nm, ∼1.9 nm, and ∼3.4 nm respectively. In this approach, a highly porous metal–organic framework (MOFs) template, rich in reticular pores of 1–10 nm is used for QDs synthesis. In template-assisted synthesis, a soluble template is dipped and filled with precursor and kept at a high temperature of around 200 °C. The temperature and incubation time may vary with the precursor that allows for settling of solution. The next step is the removal of the template without disturbing QDs which was done by solvent washing (Rasal et al., 2021).

Mesoporous silica is prepared from tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as the precursor in presence of N-hexadecylamine as a surfactant. Thus, prepared silico template is used for QDs synthesis from the citric acid precursor. Prepared QDs were hydrophilic, uniform in size ranging 1.5–2.5 nm offering a higher QY of around 23% (Zong et al., 2010). Yang et al. has compared the performance of soft and hard templates with different precursors. The soft template is made up of ‘Pluronic P123’ and mesoporous silica (SBA-15) was used as a hard template. Organic molecules like diaminebenzene (DAB), pyrene (PY), phenanthroline (PHA), and 1,3,5-trimethylbenzene (TMB) were used as carbon sources. It was observed that QY of synthesised QDs via template-assisted synthesis is around 3.3–4.7% which is comparable to counterparts prepared by high temperatures carbonization (Yang et al., 2013). The biggest advantage of template-assisted synthesis is the pores are uniform in shape and size but still least explored for biomass precursors. Each method has its own pros and cons (Table 1 ) hence selection of appropriate method is solely depends upon the type of precursor, required physical and chemical properties.

The behaviour of any material is strongly influenced by its structural, topographical features, and chemical composition. It becomes more prominent when it comes to small-sized particles due to increased surface area and reduced possibility of intermolecular interferences. Thus, it becomes necessary to decipher the structural and chemical characteristics in detail. For better understanding, the features have been categorized into structural, optical, and chemical and summarized below:

CDs are miniature sized particles with at least one dimension below 10 nm however there is still a chance for variability in shape and dimensions. For detailed elaboration, several techniques are available like atomic force microscopy (AFM), electron microscopy (EM), x-ray diffraction (XRD), etc. The selection of technique is depending upon the type of analysis and feature considered. For surface topography like the type of surface and distribution of particle size, AFM is a reliable method that scan sample surface. The system is equipped with a cantilever attached to piezo-actuator and a laser system, focussed at the back side of cantilever. Photo-diode read the deflection in the laser beam due to van der Waals (VWF) and repulsive forces that attract and repel it respectively (Giessibl, 2003). AFM successfully revealed the geometry, density, and surface topography of QDs with the help of atomic forces but there is no possibility to determine the compositional and chemical nature of the material used. Crystal structure with lattice packaging within a particle can be determined with XRD. As a result of interaction with the sample, x-ray generates peaks due to constructive interference of monochromatic x-ray beam at specific angles from each set of lattice planes. XRD pattern is a fingerprint pattern for crystal lattice and periodic atomic arrangements type irrespective of material (“Methods for Assessing Surface Cleanliness” 2019; Vishwakarma and Uthaman, 2020). In order to elucidate accurate particle size and surface image with elemental composition, the sample can be analysed with TEM. TEM imposes a beam of high energy electronsto pass the sample and collected by a detector located below. Recent advancements and integration of other techniques like energy dispersive x-ray spectroscopy (EDX) further enhance its applicability. TEM can elucidate crystal arrangement and position of lattice planesin solid crystals along with elemental composition (Gajjela and Koenraad, 2021; Walther, 2017).

Another method that provides high-resolution in-depth images is cross-sectional scanning tunnelling microscopy (X-STM). Itused the principle of quantum tunnelling through a vacuum to generate a sample image of atomic resolution. While scanning, tunnelling current is generated in presence of bias voltage that controls the movement of tip. The tunnelling current is extremely sensitive and its availability depends upon the distance between the sample surface and tip thus the scanning can be done either with a constant current or with constant height (Binning and Rohrer, 1993; Gajjela and Koenraad, 2021). Atom Probe Tomography (APT) combined the field ion microscope and mass spectrometer to generate a subatomic image of the sample by applying high-voltage pulses that liberate the single ions from the apex and accelerated towards the detector. The conventional APT is suitable for conducting material like metals but the addition of laser, and thermal source for excitation and integration of the concept of photoconductivity widen the range of materials. APT analysis visualizes three-dimensional tomographic images along with providing mass spectral analysis that reveals the associated chemical species. Limited dimension of analysis nominal detection efficiency (60%) that to in ideal operating conditions, and complex sample preparation makes APT analysis more tedious (Inoue et al., 2008; Müller et al., 2008; Thompson et al., 2007).

AFM and EM (TEM and SEM) are the most common method used for QDs characterization. Liang and colleagues prepared xylan based CQDs and dopped them with nitrogen (N-XyCQDs) by using NH4OH. Topographical analysis with HRTEM revealed that N-XyCQDs were spherical, having an average diameter of 6.92 ± 1.33 nm, while particle height was confirmed from AFM which was 1.19 ± 0.58. The particles having surface defects which was clear from HRTEM. XRD patterns have a distinct peak at 24° attributed toamorphous carbon (Liang et al., 2014). As revealed in TEM image analysis, neem leaves based graphite QDs were of 5–6 nm. In bare QDs, super lattice like structure was also observed due to aggregation by π- π stacking. Functionalization with amino group, prevents aggregation and superlattice formation (Suryawanshi et al., 2014). Rice husk graphite based QDs have a narrow size distribution 3–6 nm, crystalline with a lattice parameter of 0.24 nm. AFM revealed that the thickness of the sample (0.8–1.6 nm) was due to three layers of graphene. The extent of disorder is another important factor that not only determines structural but also governs optical properties. RHQDs are only made of carbon and have very high structural regularity. Raman spectra detected a very low relative intensity of the “disorder” D band which might be the reason for bluish fluorescence (Wang et al., 2016). Biomass based QDs encompasses carbon as the main backbone with oxygen, nitrogen, and other elements. Walnut shells are rich in cellulose and thus carbonized to generate carbon and subsequently for the synthesis of CQDs. CQDs are composed of plane graphite carbon with 0.258 nm lattice spacing and an average size of 3.4 nm. CQDs were hydrophilic, pH sensitive showing green fluorescence against excitation wavelength of 360–460 nm (Cheng et al., 2017).

AFM and TEM analysis of chitosan based CQDs showed a wide margin in particle size distribution between non-dopped and dopped QDs. Bare QDs have an average particle size of 9.12 nm while N-QDs were much smaller, having an average particle size of 1.43 nm. AFM histogram showed the particle height and it was suggested that particle height in both cases were almost the same. It is clear from TEM images that particles were spherical. The difference in particle size may be due to bare QDs form aggregates or undergone over-condensation which increased the overall particle size and doping with urea prevents the aggregation/over-condensation thus size was smaller and hasnarrow size distribution (Rybarczyk et al., 2021).

QDs are generally own fluorescence and photoluminescence for a specific wavelength. Optical characteristics of QDs can be determined with absorption-emission spectrometry, UV–Vis spectrophotometry, and fluorometry. In UV–Vis spectro-photometry, a light beam of 200–800 nm passed through the sample and the detector recorded the absorbance of the sample with respect to an external standard. While in fluorometry, a sample is excited with a light source of a particular wavelength range and in response, the wavelength of the emitted light is recorded. This kind of analysis is performed for the sample showing fluorescence (Adams and Barbante, 2015). The difference between the intensity of incoming intensity is used to calculate the quantum yield of QDs. Conventional photometry and spectroscopy can be used for structural and optical profiling of samples but these techniques generate a report for the whole sample. However, in nanoparticles/QDs a single particle may behave differently in terms of structure, topography, and chemical features from the whole sample and may represent heterogeneity. Recently, an advanced method ‘Single-particle spectroscopy (SPS)’ has been introduced that can focus even on a single particle. SPS is equipped with single-photon avalanche diodes, electron-multiplied charge-coupled device cameras that can record the behaviour of a single particle with the conventional instrument but with higher quantum efficiency and lower noise in comparison to conventional approaches. The main aim of SPS is to define the influence of a single particle on the whole sample, and designing of the process for nanoparticles as per requirement (homo/heterogenous) (Al-Zubeidi et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2020).

The aqueous solution of N-dopped xylan based CQDs (XyCQDs) was transparent and yellowish in visible light emitting blue fluorescence under UV radiation of 365 nm. UV-VIS spectrum N-XyCQDs have weak absorption at 273 nm with a shoulder peak at 310 nm due to π–π* transition of CC bonds and n–π* transition of CO bonds respectively. N-XyCQDs have maximum emission at 403 nm against excitation with 320 nm with QY of 16.18% while XyCQDs have only 2.06% (Liang et al., 2014). Amino functionalization of graphite QDs exhibited a blue shift in UV-VIS spectrum due to non-aggregation among QDs. Bare QDs and functionalization have preferable absorption in UV region at 300 nm due to π-π* transition (Suryawanshi et al., 2014). Fermentable sugars were extracted from sugarcane and residual biomass was used for QDs preparation by carbonization with phosphoric acid. Prepared GQDs were spherical with an average size of 2.26 nm and exhibited bright blue fluorescence. Residual polysaccharides and lignin contribute to GQDs preparation thus GQDs were hydrophilic. GQDs were pale yellow in sunlight but exhibited bright blue at 365 nm. Due to π-π* transitions and n-π* transition in CC bond is responsible for absorption peak at 280 nm and 320 nm. The absorption-emission spectrum has maximum emission at 461 nm with respect to 390 nm. The presence of “giant red-edge effect” showed the dependency of emission wavelength on the excitation wavelength. HR-TEM and AFM detected the particle height of 5 nm with a lattice space of 0.24 nm. The GQDs are stable in an aqueous solution for more than six months at room temperature. It was elucidated from XPS results that GQDs were rich in oxygen and carbon with a fraction of nitrogen (Chai et al., 2019). The UV–Visible spectrum of chitosan based CQDs (urea dopped and bare) have strong absorption in the UV region due to n–π* transitions in CO bonds. However, dopped CQDs (CSU-180) have a strong band at 287 nm in comparison to the bare one (CS-180) due to the presence of nitrogen atoms. Photoluminescence spectrometry showed that both types of particles have the same absorption wavelength at 290 nm but differ in QY. The presence of nitrogen atoms in CSU-180 increases the QY to 17% in comparison to 13% (Rybarczyk et al., 2021).

In order to use QDs as a carrier for any application, complete information of its surface chemistry is very important. The techniques employed to identify the chemical species, availability of functional groups, and elemental composition are discussed ahead. Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is an advanced instrument used to determine the elemental composition, its chemical state, and the presence of chemical species. High energy X-rays showered on sample surface under vacuum which leads to emission of electron from sample and based on the type of electrons released, and binding energy the actual elemental composition and chemical state of the compound can be determined. In addition, chemical species can be determined from peak position and shape. It is a reliable technique used to assess the chemical composition of samples however it is time-consuming and expensive like other sophisticated and advanced instruments (“Methods for Assessing Surface Cleanliness” 2019; Shard, 2020). The detailed information about functional groups and chemical bonds is provided by Fouriertransform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). It scans samples with IR radiation of broad wavenumbers range (4000 and 400 cm−1) and the detector recorded the absorbance or transmittance for each wavenumber. Based on the position and shape of bands, possible functional groups and type of bonds can be identified (Janssens, 2006; Tajik et al., 2020).

XPS pattern of xylan based CQDs (XyCQDs) confirmed the presence of C (71.68%) and O (21.95%) due to the normal structure of xylan along with 6.37% N due to the addition of NH4OH. XPS also showed the presence of Na which was due to traces, used for the treatment of biomass with NaOH. XPS spectrum has four clear signals at 284.7 eV for sp2 aromatic C–C, 285.5eV for C–N, 286.2eV for C–O. Xylan is rich in hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxylic functional groups which were also reflected in FTIR spectrum which has a band at 3650–3350/cm for O–H/N–H stretching followed by a bands at 1574 and 1400/cm attributed to overlapped CO and N–H respectively. Besides some weaker band at 1043/cm for C–C stretching (Liang et al., 2014). As observed in the case of biomass based functional groups, Suryawanshi and colleagues also reported the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and carboxyl functional groups in neem-based graphite QDs (Suryawanshi et al., 2014). FTIR spectrum of CQDs, prepared from chitosan with and without doping with urea i.e., CSU-180 and CS-180. Both the CDs exhibited band at 3000–3500/cm, referred to the presence of –OH group at surface however the strength was much weaker in CSU-180 due to doping with nitrogen which change hydroxyl group to an amide. The spectrum consists of sharp band at 1572 and 1418/cm which referred to CC of aromatic nature and C–OH bending respectively. Effect of doping with urea can be predicted from the distinct band in CSU-180 at 1288/cm for C–N, N–H vibration in amide group, and 1675/cm for –NHCO–. These bands were absent in CS-180. XPS spectrum exhibited different peak pattern in C1, N1 and O1 spectrum. C1 spectrum have convoluted peak consist of three different peaks at 284.7 eV for graphite carbon, 286.0 eV for C–O/C–N and 287.4 eV for CO. N1 and O1 spectrums confirmed the presence of pyridinic N (398.7–399.1eV), NH in amine and amide (400.1–400.2eV), protonated nitrogen/quartanery carbon (401.3–401.6 eV), OC- (531.3 eV) and -O-C- (533.2 eV) (Rybarczyk et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Factors determining QDs characteristics and tunability
ce:section.ce:para: Application of QDs depends upon their structural, optical, and chemical properties which are under influence of chemical composition, a precursor molecule, type of treatment, and conditions (Fig. 2 ). The effect of various factors on physico-chemical behaviour are discussed below:

Applications of QDs in energy conversion rely on the harvesting of energy, extraction of the electron, and lower resistance. It has been seen that size of QDs has a direct effect on optical properties of QDs like colour, type, and intensity of photoluminescence (Fig. 2a). Liu and colleagues showed that the photoluminescence intensity of QDs increased with a size reduction. In graphene QDs, reduction in size resulted in increased emission intensity and sharper emission peak. Raman spectra also support the fact as peaks of G and D band became distinct with a size reduction (Z. Liu et al., 2021).

Sarkar and colleagues evaluate the effect of particle size on optical properties of sucrose based CQDs. Sucrose solutions of the same concentration were carbonized hydrothermally with variable operating conditions i.e., temperature (120–150 °C) and time (120 and 150 min). It was observed that the size of CQDs increased from 5 to 10 nm with temperature. With respect to particle size, colour of emission changed from light purple, purple, purple-blue, blue, and deep blue to light green with maximum λ em at 280 nm against excitation wavelength 240–440 nm. The change in colour is due to sp2 clusters and a reduced energy gap (Sarkar et al., 2016). Zhu et al. also reported a similar kind of effect of particle size on CQDs fluorescence by synthesising CQDs by seeded growth method. As a result, CQDs obtained of six different sizes i.e.,1.6 ± 0.2, 3.6 ± 0.3, 4.6 ± 0.2, 5.0 ± 0.2, 5.4 ± 0.2, and 5.6 ± 0.2 nm and on increase in size the colour of phosphorescence changed from blue, green, yellow and orange. In addition, the quantum yield of CQDs also varies (17, 17, 24, 24, 26, and 26%) (Zhu et al., 2019). Papaioannou et al. also reported an increase in quantum yield with the reduction in size of glucose based QDs, synthesised by hydrothermal treatment (Papaioannou et al., 2019). QDs were prepared from bacterial cellulose by hydrothermal carbonization. Two sized particles were synthesised from bacterial cellulose i.e.2–3 nm exhibited green luminescent and particle of1–2 nm exhibited blue fluorescence. It has been seen that particle size have a direct influence on optical properties (Li et al., 2020).

Previous research suggested that particle shape, size, and topography govern the optical properties of QDs. As particle size increases, the fluorescence intensity shifted toward the weaker end. CQDs contain sp2 hybridized carbon with π-conjugated domains in the core. However, in larger sized QDs the probability of deformities increased due to the presence of sp3 hybridized carbon. With respect to surface deformities and increase in π-conjugated domain, the fluorescence colour shifted from blue, green, yellow to orange in addition to the reduction in intensity. Such behaviour can be exploited for the colour coding sensors and waste detection. Smaller sized particle has higher cumulative surface area thus increased the probability of interaction with pollutant thus might become the perfect solution for biosensor development and waste degradation.

Biomass derived carbon dots are mainly composed of carbon derived from waste material but the presence of additional elements and chemical moieties have also been reported. As mentioned in the above section, QDs can be synthesised from all kinds of waste biomass irrespective of their composition. In natural organic residues, saccharides (cellulose, hemicelluloses, starch, and sugars) and lignin are present in abundance in comparison to proteins and vitamins hence CDs are preferable to contain fractions derived from these biomolecules.

Yan and colleagues prepared CDs from starch via high-pressure carbonization at 300 °C for 4 h followed by sonication with oxidizing agents (H2O2, and acetic acid). Prepared CQDs were of 5–8 nm size offering bluish fluorescence and QY of 11.4%. CDs are mainly composed of carbon (>95%) followed by oxygen (3%) (Yan et al., 2015). Wang and colleague reported that CQDs prepared from alkali lignin via hydrothermal treatment have 1.7 nm with QY of 21% (R. Wang et al., 2019). Another research group prepared QDs from lignocellulose and lignin separately via microwave assisted carbonization. In comparison to lignocellulose, lignin bases nanostructures were of larger in size 60–200 nm while lignocellulosic residues based QDs were range between 2 and 3 nm (Si et al., 2018). It has been seen that QDs have an aromatic carboxylic group and CH stretch due to the presence of lignin while such aromatic bands are weak or absent in the case of cellulose/hemicellulose based QDs. Residual biomass from three different sources sugarcane bagasse (SB), taro peel (TP), and garlic peel (GP) was evaluated for CQDs preparation. All three residues are rich in ‘C’ ranging between 35 and 42%. The main compositional difference between residues was in nitrogen content as nitrogen content in GP 86.59% followed by TP 43.30 and SB with 14.60%. EDX also confirms the presence of other elements including Ca, Mg, Al, Si, and Fe in biomass. Under same operating conditions, the particle size range vary with biomass (SB: 8–12 nm; TP: 0–2 nm & 8–12 nm; and GP: 8–11 nm). In addition, particles also differ in QY which was maximum in the case of TP (26.20%), GP (13.80%), and SB (4.45%). The main reason behind different QY was different nitrogen content and particle size (Boruah et al., 2020).

Citric acid and glucose were conjugated with TiO2 to develop a photocatalyst. QDs from both precursors have similar topographical features but in terms of carbon arrangements,CQDs-G offered better crystallinity and less defects. PL emission spectra of P25 and its conjugates with CQDs (P25/CA-CQDs and P25/G-CQDs)revealed radiative recombination which is clear from broad emission between 350 and 550 nm. However, the intensity of conjugates is lower than pure P25. Doping of P25 with CQDs-G/P25 and CQDs-CA/P25, improved the photocatalytic degradation of phenols under UV light. The presence of CQDs improved the separation of electron-hole pairs. In comparison to citric acid, glucose-based QDs have a higher potential for photocatalytic degradation which was almost 99–100% within 6 h. It can be concluded from Raman spectra, ID/IG, and PL emission that the electron separation efficiency increased with the crystallinity of CQDs and photocatalytic efficiency (Shen et al., 2018). QDs synthesised by hydrothermal carbonization from different raw materials including fish scale, wool &pighair, cucumber juice, lemon peel waste, etc., have shown direct variation in particle size as well as QY. The particle size from the biomass range from 1 to 10 nm with QY of 8–38% (Thangaraj et al., 2019).

Biopolymers i.e., microcrystalline cellulose (MCC), furfurals, and hydroxymethylfurfural (HMF) were undergone hydrothermal carbonization, and the effect of precursors on optical properties was investigated (Fig. 2b). Under the similar operating condition, the mean particle size of QDs varies with precursor molecules. The particle size of CQDs from MCC, HMF, and furfural were 6.36 ± 0.54, 5.35 ± 0.56, and 3.94 ± 0.60 nmrespectively. The difference in degradation temperature might be the reason for different particle sizes even under the same treatment condition. The CQDs are distinct not only in particle size but also in their optical behaviour. All three QDs have absorption at 240 nm and emission at 360 nm but vary significantly in intensity as MCC has the maximum intensity of 2.35 followed by 1.45 for HMF and 0.7 for furfural (Hasan et al., 2021).

To avoid harsh and expensive treatment procedures for QDs synthesis, Jing et al. proposed a mid-oxidation method for QDs synthesis from biomass using alkali and hydrogen peroxide. Biomass was carbonized hydrothermally and processed to separate biochar, hydrochar, and CQDs. Obtained hydrochar again processed with alkali and H2O2 to transform it QDs. In this way, waste hydrochar can also be used as raw material for QDs synthesis under mild oxidation that too offers higher quantum yield. Like normal QDs, it can also be used for the same applications like metal detection and bioremediation (Jing et al., 2019). Biomass and natural biomolecules including proteins, carbohydrates, and fats are rich in hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups viz –COOH, –OH, =CO, and –CHO with a small fraction of –NH2. The composition of QDs varies with the method followed for synthesis. When precursors are treated at a higher temperature under extreme conditions, QDs are rich in carbon while in milder conditions other elements may also present. Therefore, functional moieties on CQDs surface are enriched with carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups. The functional properties of CQDs are also aligned with the chemical characteristics of these functional groups. The net charge in carbonyl and hydroxyl functional groups changes with pH of the medium and have a high tendency to lose proton thus CQDs also exhibit a change in surface chemistry and show an affinity for metal ions. Such behaviour of QDs is fit for biosensor development for metal ions that can detect even a minute quantity of metal ions in a water sample.

Operating conditions play a crucial role in determining the physical, chemical, and optical properties of QDs. However, the major route for the QDs preparation is carbonization but the method followed, and the chemical involved affect the availability of functional groups, particle size, and shape. QDs exhibited enormous variability in characteristics even when they are prepared with the same raw material/precursor, even for the same method operated at different parameters. Wheat residual biomass processed by hydrothermal (S. Liu et al., 2021; Yuan et al., 2015) and pyrolysis (J. Chen et al., 2021) for QDs preparation. The effect of different reaction conditions on characteristics of QDs are summarized below:

Temperature: The idea behind the QDs preparation is to carbonize the material which means the breaking of bonds and reduce the cluster size to minimal interaction in addition to removal of any volatile material. For this purpose, different heating methods have been screened. It is suggested that not only the temperature range but also the method used for the purpose affect the final product. Direct pyrolysis, arc discharge, laser ablation is some of the methods followed to carbonize the material in which pyrolysis is commonly used. In terms of applicability, the intensity of photoluminescence is one of the major criteria for comparative analysis. Almost all the QDs and CDs were having photoluminescence that can be used for bioimaging. Yuan and colleagues prepared QDs at 250 °C were of 1.7 nm (average diameter). QDs were negatively charged exhibiting blue photoluminescence with maximum emission at 418 nm with respect to excitation by 364 nm. QDs were rich in nitrogen along with carbon (Yuan et al., 2015). While QDs prepared at 180 °C have a little larger size 5.7 nm composed of carbon mainly. QDs exhibited maximum emission at 470 nm with respect to 370 nm excitation and offered a QY of 7.5% (S. Liu et al., 2021). In contrast to hydrothermal treatment, QDs prepared by pyrolysis at 900 °C were rich in carbon followed by nitrogen. Size distribution range for QDs was 2–11 nm showing maximum emission at 425 nm against 260 nm. The QY of QDs was 28.9% which was much higher than hydrothermal (J. Chen et al., 2021). The same kind of variation was reported in the case of QDs prepared from rice straw by pyrolysis (X. Zhang et al., 2020), microwave assisted carbonization (Si et al., 2018), and hydrothermal treatment (W. Wang et al., 2018), etc. It can be interpreted that at a higher temperature, QDs particle size moves toward the lower range of scale with carbon as a major fraction while at a lower temperature range, the trend reverses along with a change in composition. At lower temperature other elements also present with carbon. The major effect of particle size reflects in optical behaviour of QDs as with particle size the nature and intensity of emission also changes and thus its application.

Solvent: Solvo-synthesis is well-known approach for bottom-up synthesis of QDs. As discussed in the ultrasonication-assisted synthesis of QDs, the presence of solvent modulates the surface tension, viscosity, and density of the solution, and hence the mass and energy transfer rate. In a highly viscous medium mass transfer is slow but heat generation is high due to increased inter-layer friction. A higher concentration of solute also reduces the surface tension and stabilizes the nano-particles. The addition of solvents with lower surface tension or surfactants increases the stability of nanostructures (Kumar et al., 2020). Liang reported that the presence of solvents may improve the fluorescence level in QDs while working on agricultural and forestry residues as a precursor (Liang et al., 2014). These residues are rich in xylan hence used as model precursors. For comparative evaluation, xylan was dissolved in water and NH4OH followed by hydrothermal treatment. Here NH4OH acted as a passivation agent as well as a source of nitrogen for doping. Under the same operating conditions, NCQDs were smaller (6.9 nm) than CQDs (19.06 nm) and spherical. Both types of CQDs (Nitrogen dopped: N-CQDs and non-dopped: CQDs) exhibited high-quality blue fluorescence however QY and intensity was much different. In comparison to CQDs, the QY of NCQDs was much higher which was around 16% but the yield of CQDs was more in the case of CQDs (95%) than NCQDs (22.4%) (Liang et al., 2014). Besides synthesis, the presence of solvents also affects the performance of QDs and aid in the fabrication of nano-devices. It was suggested that the effect of solvent on the physical and optical properties of QDs depends upon the nature of the solvent and its chemical interaction with solvents. Walnut shell CQDs reduced the photoluminescence in presence of alcohol as a solvent in comparison to water. CQDs are hydrophilic and have high solubility in water but in presence of alcohol, CQDs tend to form aggregates. In aggregates CQDs undergone self-quenching of fluorescence and thus the intensity of fluorescence is reduced which can be restored in water (Cheng et al., 2017). Similarly, CDs prepared from 1,3,6-trinitropyrene (TNP) were analysed for photoluminescence with respect to solvents. TNP-CDs was prepared in toluene as solvent by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 12 h. Prepared CDs were of 2.66 nm, soluble in organic solvents. Post-synthesis, CDs were dissolved in different solvents toluene (TOL), ethyl acetate (EA), acetone (ACT), and dimethyl fumarate (DMF) (based on the polarity) separately and evaluated for photoluminescence performance. The fluorescence of TNP-CDs reduced from the polarity of solvents from non-polar to polar. Absorption maximum changes with respect to solvents as for CDs solution i.e., TOL-TNP-CDs, EA-TNP-CDs, ACT-TNP-CDs, and DMF-TNP-CDs the absorption λ max shifted from 575 nm to 620 nm, 630 nm, and 715 nm respectively. QY also followed a similar pattern as QY reduced from 65.93% to 50.61, 35.01, and 9.47% respectively (Wu et al., 2017). It can be interpreted that use of solvents while QDs preparation affects the surface morphology, charge, and functional moieties. It also influences the solubility of QDs thus fluorescence properties in solvents are affected which means hydrophilic QDs exhibited higher performance in polar solvents while hydrophobic QDs in organic and non-polar solvents. The properties can be utilized for the fabrication of catalytic systems that selectively modulated performance with respect to the type of phase. Like, hydrophilic QDs can be used for the detection of organic pollutants and vice versa.

p H : pH refers to the strength of H+ and OH− ions in solvents containing precursor molecules while QDs synthesis. In the case of biomass as a precursor, the optimum pH range depends upon the composition of biomass or biomass fraction or selected biomolecules. Like proteins changes their electronic state and net charge with respect to pH of the medium. The effect of pH on QDs behaviour is totally depends upon the composition and surface functional groups. CQDs prepared from watermelon peels were rich in carbon followed by oxygen and nitrogen. Due to the presence of polar functional groups, QDs were hydrophilic and exhibit pH sensitivity in optical behaviour. CQDs showed strong bluish fluorescence which is stable over a wide range of pH (2.0–11.0) (Fig. 2c). The fluorescence was significantly stable even against high salt concentrations (Zhou et al., 2012). In contrast to this, some of the QDs performance prefer either acidic or alkaline pH. As seen in the case of QDs prepared from coconut husk prepared by hydrothermal treatment contained a huge amount of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface which get protonated and deprotonated at different pH and thus exhibits a change in net surface charge. With respect to change in charge the fluorescence from the QDs also changes. In moving the pH scale from acidic to alkaline the intensity of fluorescence get decreased (Chunduri et al., 2016). Similarly, QDs prepared from eggshell membranesexhibited fluorescence in the alkaline pH range. The intensity of fluorescence increased with respect to pH scale move from 7.0 to 11.0 (Wang et al., 2012). Kumar et al. reported QDs from Ocimum sanctum that showed maximum fluorescence at neutral pH. Fluorescence intensity increased when pH increased from 2.0 to 7.0 but further increase in pH resulted in reduced fluorescence (Kumar et al., 2017).

In most cases, biomass based QDs prefer neutral pH for their activity but exhibit pH dependent fluorescence in colour as well as intensity. The pH sensitive nature of QDs makes them eligible to use in pH-based detection and develop pH-based biosensors.

The surface structure of QDs determines their properties. For example, surface defects and functional groups determine the intensity and colour of photoluminescence (Barman and Patra, 2018). QDs prepared from natural biomass are rich in oxygen along with carbon but may also accompanyby nitrogen (N) and sulphur (S). The presence of different atoms determines the energy levels and possible electronic transition thus the colour of photoluminescence (Yi et al., 2019). In CQDs, the core is made of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms surrounded by sp3 hybridized carbon. Due to the presence of other atoms, carbon acts as a trapping centerfor energy (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021). Apart from surface defects, the availability of functional groups also plays a crucial role. Quantum dots synthesised from natural waste material are rich in oxygen-containing functional groups that provide functional sites for interaction and attachment. However, in some cases, QDs need surface modification and activation to add more sites and to create diversity in functional groups for the attachment of other bioactive molecules. Surface functionalization of QDs facilitates by different chemical interactions like covalent bonding; π–π interactions; coordination; and sol–gel technology. Surface modification and doping are one of the most important steps to make QDs and nanomaterials fit for the specific application. Surface modification can be differentiated into surface functionalization and hetero-atom doping.

Doping refers to the integration of atoms other than main constituents which have been added during synthesis and it becomes a part of QDs structure. Most importantly, doping can modulate the inherent features of QDs like photoluminescence and QY apart fromthe structure. Another advantage of doping is the participation of dopants in electron shifting and energy harvesting (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021). Doping is basically a creation of surface defects in carbon structure with the addition of other elements which may be metals or non-metals. The type of defects created by the dopant depends upon the size of the dopant and its electronic configuration and the defects are usually associated with HOMO-LUMO energy gaps. Doping can change the photoluminescence behaviour, improve the intensity of fluorescence and increase the quantum yield (Fig. 3 ).

Usually, nitrogen, phosphorus, and sulphur are commonly used for doping (Barman and Patra, 2018). It has been observed that doping of QDs with sulphur exhibited a red shift in photoluminescence while doping with nitrogen showed a blue shift (Abbas et al., 2018). Nitrogen is the most common element used for QDs doping due to comparable atomic configuration and it can easily integrate with carbon atom chain without any replacement. As a result of nitrogen doping wavelength of emission resulted in blue shift (Khairol Anuar et al., 2021; Zhu et al., 2019). In contrast to nitrogen, doping with phosphorus creates a substitution defect as phosphorus is larger than nitrogen. In quantum dots core sp2 hybridized carbon is surrounded by sp3 hybridized atoms and phosphorus substitute sp3 hybridized atoms and acts as n-type donor (Zhou et al., 2012).

Sulphur-doping adds new energy level in quantum dots electronic configuration between already available energy levels i.e. π-π* and compensates the band gap thus dopped QDs exhibited tunable multiple emissions peaks (Li et al., 2014). It has been observed that not only dopant but also its quantity affects the physiological behaviour of QDs. Table 2 summarizes the effect of doping agents on the optical behaviour of QDs.

Surface functionalization suggests adding specific functional group/s to the surface of QDs. Each precursor has its own arrangement of functional groups which is reflected on the surface of QDs. In addition, some functional groups can also be added to the surface as attachments utilizing the available functional moieties as attachment sites. Availability and type of functional groups i.e. hydrophilic and hydrophobic determine the dispersion rate of QDs in aqueous and non-aqueous solvents (Abbas et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 3, surface modifications assisted with different physical and chemical interactions. The type of modification and involved interactions depend upon available functional moieties on QDs surface and incoming function groups. The basic concept behind the interaction is common chemistry viz type of chemical reaction or affinity function groups are showing.

Surface functionality changes with controlled oxidation which led to change in optical and chemical behaviour. Reduction in oxygenated surface, QY of CQDs increases while in increasing oxidation level, photoluminescence exhibited red shift (Abbas et al., 2018). The fact was also supported by the reduction of GQDs with NaBH4 which resulted in blue-shifted in photoluminescence as well as double the QY (Li et al., 2012). Photoluminescence of CQDs changed from green to blue when the carboxylic group is replaced with alkylamine. Several functional groups have been identified which affect the optical characteristics of QDs. The addition of alkylamine, diamine, hydrazide, and thiol groups to CQDs surface enhances the QY (Abbas et al., 2018). CDs were prepared from citric acid and thiourea by hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C and modified with citric acid and poly (ethyleneimine) separately. As a result of surface modification, particle size increased from 3.8 nm to 5.0 and 13.6 nm respectively. Peroxidase activity of all three CDs was evaluated against 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and 2,2-azinobis (3-ethyl -benzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid) diammonium salt (ABTS) as substrates. The catalytic activity of CA-CDs was much higher for TMB while for ABTS the activity of PEI-CDs was higher. This difference in activity was due to differential interaction with CDs viz CA-CDs is negatively charged and TMB has a higher affinity for negatively charged moiety and ABTS showed stronger interaction with positive charge thus have an affinity for PEI-CDs (Shi et al., 2019).

The major trend with surface modification and hetero-atom doping showed that the availability of different functional groups at the surface and the presence of hetero-atoms govern the physical and physiological characters of QDs. Net negative charge and polarity of QDs is a critical feature for its application and interaction with other elements and compounds. The addition of heteroatoms and functional groups can modulate its functionality. Due to such behaviour of QDs, it is a suitable candidate for waste degradation, environmental bioremediation, and biosensor development. QDs can be made target-specific based on the affinity and electronic interaction i.e., covalent and noncovalent type to act precisely.

QDs prepared from organic biomass (agricultural and industrial biomass) provide enumerable opportunities for industrial application (Table 3 ). The main focus of attraction is asmall size, and availability of sites for the attachment of other functional groups in addition to fluorescence emission that increased its applicability. To date, researches have shown the potential use of CQDs in chemical sensing, healthcare, sensor development, bio-imaging, labelling, and catalysis apart from environmental remediation. QDs have fluorescence with respect to its size, backbone structure, and surface functional groups hence any change in surface characteristics resulted in modulation of fluorescence/visible signals. This feature of CQDs allows its application in chemical sensing like metal ions, dyes, colours, pollutants (Meng et al., 2019; Y. Wang et al., 2018). In addition, QDs are resistant to photo-bleaching and there is the least possibility for toxic effect on a biological system. High cellular uptake is one of the major advantages for the QDs hence these are a suitable candidate for bio-imaging, labelling, and drug delivery. This potential has already been validated successfully in various cell lines, Zebrafish embryo, and other experimental models (Chahal et al., 2021; Q. Zhang et al., 2019). The present review is especially dedicated to its application in energy and environment conservation. In the upcoming section, special attention is paid to its utility in bioenergy and environmental conservation.

Energy is a basic need for every industrial sector as well as household activities (Bhatia et al., 2020a). Growing energy demand also fuelled the need of highly efficient conversion, storage, and transfer systems (Sekar et al., 2021). Integration of quantum dots in energy sectors suggested the reduction in the use of toxic chemicals and metal and their discharge. The uses of QDs in the energy sector are discussed below as per their applications in the fabrication of conversion (solar cells), storage (capacitors and batteries), and use (LEDs) (Fig. 4 ):

Solar cell: Solar light is a renewal and green source for power that can be transformed to electricity (Fig. 4a). Conversion of solar to electricity depends upon electron transfer efficiency. Reduction in resistance and increase in charge transfer improve the efficiency of output power. QDs can facilitate charge transfer and aid in designing an efficient solar cell and other electricity storage devices. Previous literature showed the efficiency of silicon-based solar photovoltaics around 17–25%. However, thinning of the silicon layer and modification of structure increased the light-harvesting rate and photo-conversion efficiency. The use of silicon in solar cells construction reduced chemical wastage due to low-toxicity and abundance. Replacement of silicon layering or integration of optically active nanostructures (STs) including QDs, and other nanostructures improved solar cell performance by 60% in ultraviolet radiation. An increase in energy conversion is evidently justified with a change in the bandgap. Bandgap for Si is around 1.12 eV while for SiNTs 1.8 eV thus it can utilize higher energy wavelength as well. Farirai and colleagues reviewed natural sources especially agricultural residues for Si for QDs synthesis and showed rice crop residues for silica and silicon. However, biomass must be treated at a higher temperature range of around 500–700 °C (Farirai et al., 2021).

In solar cells, electrodes are usually coated with sensitizer dyes that improve the charge transfer and activity of electrodes. In order to reduce the cost of solar cells and the application of synthetic sensitizers, biomass-based CDs were used. Briscoe and colleagues coated zinc rods with CDs of chitin, chitosan and glucose by soaking while solar cell construction. The presence of CDs affected the efficiency of the solar cell due to increased light harvesting and increased surface area. The increase in efficiency of solar cell for chitin and chitosan were higher in comparison to glucose (Briscoe et al., 2015). Availability of sufficient energy/solar radiation is another factor apart from charge separation and electron extraction that hinder the solar to electricity conversion efficiency. Meng et al. designed all weather solar cells with CQDs from soybean powder. The aqueous solution of soybean powder undergone hydrothermal treatment at 170 °C. Solar cells are constructed by incorporating CQDs with TiO2 anode incorporated with CQDs to capture solar energy of higher as well as low intensity and even in dark. The designed solar cell offered maximized dark power conversion efficiency of 7.97% (Meng et al., 2017). Similarly, CDs, prepared from lotus root (LR) and doped with nitrogen and sulphur in solar cell construction. Functionalized LR-QDs were utilized as light absorbers in mesoscopic solar cells and efficiency was assessed in terms of light harvesting and electron extraction. The use of LR-QDs as bi- and tri-cascaded photovoltaics increased the power conversion efficiencies by 0.158% and 0.208% respectively. Further application of QDs as a sensitizer with N719 dyes increased the efficiency by 9.04% (Liu et al., 2019). In conventional solar cells, TiO2 photoanodes are coated with graphene quantum dots or sensitizer dyes which suffer from lower conversion efficiency. To overcome the issue, graphene QDs were prepared with cellulose and used alone and in combination with sensitizer dye N719 by ‘layer by layer’ and mixture fabrication. In comparison to conventional architects, solar cell equipped with GQDs and dye as layer by layer and mixture offered higher efficiency under low light as well as a standard light. Maximum efficiency (%), fill factor (%), and maximum power (mW/cm2) for layer by layer configuration offered were 16.76, 45.65, and 4.189 followed by 12.07, 92.63, and 3.014 respectively in case of a mixture under low light. Similar parameters under standard light were2.91, 75.97, and 2.906 respectively for mixture while 1.43, 62.04, and 1.425 for layer by layer arrangement. In brief use of cellulose GQD with N719 dye improved the performance of cell (Mahalingam et al., 2021). Several other researchers also suggested the increase in efficiency of solar cells and photovoltaics due to the integration of QDs.

Supercapacitors: Energy demand and increasing environmental concern boost up the use of storage devices that can store a large amount of energy and supply when needed. Capacitors and batteries are among such devices that can store electrical energy. With the passage of time the requirement of high energy capacitors increased in hybrid cars, electronic devices, and memory storage devices, thus conventional fabrication improved for storage capacity and introduce supercapacitors (SC) with high energy density (>10 kW/kg) and longer lifecycles (>105 cycles). SCs consists of two electrodes and a medium lectrolyte phase in between. Based on the mechanism of storage SCs are classified into three types i.e.,electric-double-layer capacitors (EDLC), pseudocapacitors, and hybrid types. In EDLC SCs, electrostatic charge accumulated in electrolyte between electrodes. EDLCs type SCs are stable and can be used for longer duration (usually infinite time of charge/discharge cycles) however the charge density is low due to limited useable surface area. The second type of SCs is ‘Pseudocapacitors’ in which reversible faradaic redox is used to store energy in the electrolyte phase.
