Pseudocapacitors offer high specific capacitance but suffer fromalimited life cycle. The third type of fabrication involved hybrid type ‘hybrid SCs'in which the advantage of both SCs can be utilized (Permatasari et al., 2021).

To improve the stability and charge density, QDs are integrated into SCs fabrication which increased charge density. Lemon juice CQDs (LJ-CQDs), prepared by the hydrothermal method were used to fabricate supercapacitors. Incorporation of a composite of nickel sulfide nanostructure with LJ-CQDs in SCs improved specific storage capacity to 880 F/g when charged with a current density of 2 A/g. In addition, the SCs are stable up to 2000 cycles (charge–discharge cycles). In comparison to conventional SCs fabrication with graphene/Ni saltoffer a charge density of 10.8 Wh/Kg which increased to 30 Wh/Kg when nickel/CQDs composite was used in fabrication (Sahoo et al., 2018). Reduced graphene oxide (RGO) composite was prepared with CDs, synthesised from cauliflower leaf wastes, and evaluated for electrochemical performance. Incorporation of CDs in composite, increased the surface area and pore volume in comparison to RGO alone as pore volume (cm3/g) increased from 0.96 to 2.23, 2.94, and finally to 3.01 in GO:CD ratio of 1:0, 4:1, 2:1, and 1:1 respectively. Likewise, surface area (m2/g) also increased in the same pattern from 191 to 373, 415, and finally to 487. In electrochemical performance composite of 2:1GO:CD offered maximum specific capacitance as well as maximum discharge capacitances of 278 F/g and 227 F/g respectively with respect to 0.2 A/g and 2 mV/s respectively. Even at a higher current density of 100 A/g, SC retained 60% capacitance (Hoang et al., 2019). Recently composite of CDs, prepared from carrot with polyaniline, polypyrrole, and graphene was used to fabricate SCs with high performance and high charge density. The addition of all three has a synergistic effect on the electrochemical performance of SC as fabricated SC exhibited maximum specific capacitance of 396 F/g that retained 62% capacitance at a current density of 5 A/g (Oskueyan et al., 2021). Wang et al. used Ti3C2Tx-based hybrid fibre modified by nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) to fabricate a photo assisted charging fibrous supercapacitor (NM2P1) and NM2P1 fibre able to provide a volumetric capacitance of 1445 F cm−3 which was 35.9% higher than under dark conditions (Fig. 4b) (H. Wang et al., 2021).

Batteries: Batteries are also energy storage devices like capacitors that have shown immense potential in countering the imbalance between energy demand and supply. It is a portable source of electrical energy with a long-life cycle. The basic architecture of batteries and capacitors are more or less the same as it also consists of two electrodes that emerged in electrolytes in which energy is stored in form of charge. Conventional models use lithium ion batteries due to low weight, less redox potential, and small size of ion which allow easy and rapid movement while charging and discharging. Integration of QDs might be one of the safest and green approaches to improve the efficiency of conventional batteries. CQDs can be used for electrode/anode construction which intercalate Li+ in nano as well as micropores along with surface deformities and by maintaining proper interlayer distance to improve the efficiency without disturbing the movement of ions (Thangaraj et al., 2021b).

As discussed above, biomass is a rich and renewable source for carbon based QDs that can be used in batteries fabrication as well. Graphite was prepared with carbon extracted from banana peel for the upgradation of sodium ion battery (NIB) and lithium ion battery (LIB) anodes. Banana peel was carbonized at 800, 1100, and 1400 °C to extract carbon. Extracted carbon seemed promising for electrode fabrication due to low surface areas of 19–217 m2/g and a high packing density of 0.75 g/cm3 in comparison to 1 g/cm3 for conventional graphite. The electrochemical behaviour was evaluated against sodium ion batteries and batteries with banana peel graphite offered a capacity of 355 mAh/g even after 10 cycles at a charge density of 50 mA/g. Integration of QDs improved the coulombic efficiency of batteries. Further increase in pyrolysis temperature from 800 to 1100 °C, improved the coulombic efficiency from 63% to 73%. In the case of carbon extracted at 800 and 1100 °C, the CE increased to 98% after 5 cycles and 100% after 10 cycles and can work for more than 600 cycles (Lotfabad et al., 2014). Nan and colleagues prepared CQDs from hulless barley straw and wheat straw and processed them for composite formation with Al(NO3)3·9H2O, MnO2, LiOH·H2O, and TiO2. The composite was used to fabricate anode and used in Li + ion batteries. Doping with Mn, Al and Li improved the electrical properties in terms of conductivity and energy storage. The capacity of batteries with LTO, LTO-Al-Mn, and LTO-Al-Mn-CQDs was 169.4, 240.1, and 296.5 respectively. The co-dopped anode has a specific capacity of 295.8–233.8 mAh/g which is stable even after many cycles (Nan et al., 2019). In another work, CQDs, prepared with Samanea saman leaves were used to fabricate flexible Na + batteries. QDs have maximum excitation wavelength at 360 nm and offered QY of 21.03%. Composite of CQDs with SnO2 and NaVO3 use to fabricate electrodes with different separators like indium-doped tin oxide/polyoxyethylene tridecyl ether (ITO/PTE), rice paper (RP), silicone with large holes (SIL-1), silicone with small holes (SIL-2) and cellulose paper (CP). Among all separators, SIL-2 offered specific capacitance of 881 F/g which was much higher than others. Small holes in SIL-2 provide sufficient space for the movement of ions. Batteries with SIL-2 offered maximum discharge capacities of 141 and 114 mC/g at 1.5 and 2.5 V. RP also exhibited significant performance, displayed specific discharge capacities of 1087 and 347 mC/g in 1st and 50th cycles at 1 V. The discharge capacity of SIL-2 was higher than RP, and delivered high specific discharge capacity of 4246 in 1st cycle at 2 V but maintained a capacity of 71 mC/g in the 50th cycle (Thangaraj et al., 2021a).

Light-emitting diode : Light-emitting diodes are the conversion and responsive application of CQDs where energy is reflected in form of light. In the above section, we have already mentioned the application of CQDs where the light energy can be converted to electrical energy (solar cell) and stored (capacitors and batteries) however the efficient use of energy is also necessary to reduce the energy wastage and efficient utilization. LEDs are one of the most efficient light emitters among the class with compatible emissions. In conventional models, three types of arrangements are possible with light emitting chips and phosphor.

As shown in Fig. 4c, RGBc model relies on chips emitting red, blue, and green light that combine and emit white light. In the second type of arrangement i.e., BcYP, red and green chips are replaced with a yellow phosphor. Blue chips emit blue light which excites the yellow phosphor and emission combined with blue light to emit white light from the lamp. In the third type of arrangement, UVc-RGBf all three i.e., red, blue, and green chips are replaced with a single UV chip and equipped with fluorescent materials. UV radiation emitted by UV lamp excites fluorescent material and as a result respective types of emission recorded from the lamp. All three models have respective pros and cons like differences in intensity, and efficiency, etc. The third arrangement might be a suitable system for the integration of CDs to improve the efficiency of LEDs. Besides small size, resistance to photobleaching, easy tunability, the wide colour of photoluminescence, and energy responsive nature makes them a perfect material for the fabrication of light emitting devices. The Discovery of another mechanism of luminescence i.e., electroluminescence further supportsits candidature. In electroluminescence, CQDs emit luminescence in response to electrons and holes generated from cathode and anode. Previous results suggested that CQDs are equally compatible and efficient in LED fabrication (Cui et al., 2017).

Perikala and Bhardwaj prepared CQDs from citric acid by solvothermal route in a colloidal system using octadecane as solvent. CQDs were of 4 nm and crystalline however the crystal structure varies with the operating conditions as at 300 °C, the CQDs were onion ring like while at lower temperature parallel lattice planes were observed. CQDs emit bluish green photoluminescence which was stable in an open environment for more than 9 days. CQDs have a wide absorption range for light from 830 to 300 nm without any specific maximum absorption wavelength and emission ranges from 300 to 600 nm with maximum emission at 471 nm. The QY of bare CQDs was 4% which was improved to 31% after surface functionalization with hexadecyl amine (HDA). The wide range of absorption was mainly because of the wide particle size distribution. The PL can be controlled with dilutions and with the help of surface modification. The work emphasized on its use in photonic devices (Perikala and Bhardwaj, 2019). CDs was prepared from mango leaves extract showed temperature-dependent photoluminescence as emission peak observed at 550 nm which was shifted to 430 nm upon increasing the reaction temperature from 120 °C to 150 °C. In addition, CDs emission spectrum consists of multiple bands at 411 nm, 480 nm, and 530 nm respectively and hence the colour of emission was also changed from blue to yellow. Leaves extract also contain chlorophyll molecules which added red fluorescence at 670 nm. The overall emission from the CDs was white. The prepared CDs were coated over a commercial LED chip of 365 nm. In a trial, CDs emitted greenish white light with CIE coordinates of (0.37, 0.44) (Gong et al., 2020). In another effort, biomass based solid state fluorescent-CDs were prepared fromdehydroabietic acid, a terpenoid with ethanolamine by hydrothermal route at 200 °C for 300 min. CDs prepared from terpenoid exhibited bright light-yellow fluorescence with QY of 10%. It has maximum emission at 433 nm against an excitation wavelength of 300–400 nm. CDs have graphitic carbon core decorated with variety of functional groups that contribute to optical and chemical behaviour. The CDs were embedded on blue LED chip of 450 nm with epoxy resins (3:1). Modified LED chip emitted white light with CIE coordinates of (0.3304, 0.3055) andcorrelated colour temperature (CCT) of 5608 K and colour rendering index (CRI) of 88.6 when operated at the supply of 20 mA (Ge et al., 2021).

Hydrogen evolution: In the last few years, the world is facing rising demand for energy for transport, household activities, communication, etc. (Bhatia et al., 2018; Yadav et al., 2021). Rapid and excessive consumption of conventional sources of energy and fuels increased the pollution and leading to the exhaustion of conventional energy sources (Arora et al., 2021; Bhatia et al., 2021b). Therefore, the world is looking for new and clean fuel and renewable sources of energy like biodiesel (Bhatia et al., 2021a), bioelectricity (Gurav et al., 2019), biohydrogen, etc., (Rathour et al., 2018). Among clean and renewable energy sources, hydrogen is considered as the cleanest form of energy and can be produced using different methods like dark fermentation, electrolysis, and thermochemical conversion (Bhatia et al., 2021c; Kumar et al., 2021), etc. In simple terms, electrocatalytic spitting of water releases hydrogen (Dange et al., 2021). To fulfill the purpose, platinum (Pt) is used as a catalyst that induces the splitting and generates hydrogen, but Pt is one of the most expensive catalysts which increases the production cost. For cost-effective production, Pt can be replaced with Pt-based alloys, iron, nickel, cobalt, copper, and molybdenum but suffers from limited catalytic performance and product yield. In the search for an inexpensive hydrogen evolution process, QDs also joined the group of catalysts. In the same line, metal hybrids were prepared with renewable and low-cost carbon QDs offer green, and chemically stable alternate for hydrogen evolution (Fig. 4d) (Li et al., 2019).

Wei and colleagues prepared N-dopped CQDs from shrimp biomass by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 12 h. Prepared CQDs have an average diameter of 2.5 nm and showed excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence emission. It has strong absorption over a broad range of 200 nm–1000 nm. XPS and FTIR data confirmed the presence of nitrogen in the carbon skeleton due to the presence of protein and amino acids. Further, prepared CQDs were analysed for gas production from formic acid under sunlight as energy source. It was noted that, no hydrogen was generated in the absence of a catalyst. The rate of hydrogen evolution increased upon increasing the concentration of FA but only up to 2 mL. It may be possible that available nitrogen in CQDs facilitates the dissociation of O–H bond in FA and generate hydrogen (Wei et al., 2015). In a study, ruthenium (Ru) metal nanoparticle was supported on CQDs prepared from porphyra biomass. CQDs emitted bluish green fluorescence in presence of UV radiation 365 nm and have strong emission at 450 nm. The catalytic performance of Ru-CQDs, Ru/C, and Ru-AC were evaluated and compared for HER in 1 M KOH in a standard three electrode system. Among all the catalysts, Ru/C (5 wt%) offered the overpotential of 94 mV. In comparison, Ru@CQDs prepared at an annealing temperature of 800 °C offered improved HER catalytic activity of 63 mV followed by 89 mV for Ru@PC and 127 mV for Ru@P-AC (Li et al., 2019). CDs were prepared from renewable lignocellulosic waste and used in combination with Ni-based co-catalyst for hydrogen production under visible light as an energy source. The presence of organic pollutants in crude river and seawater can act as electron donors for photoexcited CDs and facilitateproton reduction. The system converts energy from waste biomass followed by wastewater treatment and contributes to environmental conservation (Achilleos et al., 2020). Wang et al. also reported that ‘N’ and ‘S’ dopped CDs in presence of transition/trace metals improve the efficiency of hydrogen evolution rate due to synergistic effect of metal and CDs and offered the smallest value of overpotential i.e. 10 mV (Q. Wang et al., 2021). Likewise, TiO2 composite with lignocellulosic biomass derived QDs improve the hydrogen evolution efficiency to 2.43 mmol/gh and glucose to arabinose conversion selectivity of ∼75% (Zhao et al., 2022). The basic mechanism behind the hydrogen production is the available heteroatoms and QDs act for energy capture and facilitate movement of electrons which govern the proton reduction, thus improve the evolution of hydrogen.

Oil extraction and recovery: Crude oil is a conventional source of energy that is utilized in the various energy sector around the world (Quraishi et al., 2021). Most of the crude oil reservoirs are exhausted and to meet the growing oil demands world is looking for more efficient methods to recover oil from depleted and marginal reservoirs. Oil spillage is another issue associated with oil extraction from reservoirs which not only lead to environmental pollution but also resulted in an economic loss (Gurav et al., 2021). Marine organisms are the biggest sufferers of oil spillage and it also disrupt ecological framework of water bodies (Singh et al., 2020). In such conditions oil extraction process must be efficient that could extract the high amount of oil from deep reservoirs and prevent spillage and wastage in any form. In addition, a strategy must be devised that can improve the separation of water and oil while recovery from spillage.

Singh et al. have proposed the use of magnetic nanoparticles prepared from iron oxide for the recovery of oil from spillage sites (Singh et al., 2020). In the same line, QDs from renewable and biodegradable materials like organic biomass and natural biomolecules have gained much attention. Lei and colleagues fabricated a hydrophobic membrane with surface functionalized CQDs and an organic compound tolylene-2,4-diisocyanate used in textile industries for the production of polyurethane. CQDs were prepared from citric acid and functionalized with C18 hydrocarbon octadecylamine. The hydrophobic membrane was proposed to use for oil separation from the oil-water mixture as well as desalination of seawater. Membrane specifically accepted oil with less response to water due to difference in contact angles (CA). Membrane shared CA of 152.2° with water while 0p with oil thus have very less chance for water acceptance due to reduced surface area and retained water on the surface due to hydrophobic nature. Chemically the hydrophilic chemical bonds like –OH was replaced with –NCO during cross-linking. Membrane retained the same contact angles with oil and water for more than 30 cycles and offered a separation efficiency of 98%. Likewise, the desalination performance was also around 99% which suggested the commendable performance of membrane (Lei et al., 2019). Hybrid QDs were reported to improve oil recovery by modulating the interfacial surface tension and wettability. N-dopped CQDs were prepared with citric acid and emulsified in different ratio with molybdenum nanoparticles by sodium dodecyl benzyl sulfonate (SDBS) and hexadecyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB). The prepared emulsions 10% N-CQDs/MQDs were suitable to use with cationic surfactant while 50% GQDs/MQDs are suitable with an anionic surfactant. Both hybrids increased the interfacial tension (IFT) by 70% and reduced the surface tension. Also leads to a change in contact angles of oil with rocks to improve the wettability and facilitated the higher recovery of oil (22%) that to at very low nanofluid concentration (0.01 wt%) (AfzaliTabar et al., 2020). A continuous method for S-dopped CQDs was proposed by Baragau et al. for enhanced oil recovery. CQDs were prepared with glucose as precursor and surface functionalized with SCX-4. The particle size of CQDs was ranged between 1 and 2.5 nm with an average particle size of 1.7 nm with a majority around 1.5 nm. QDs were composed of sp2 hybridized carbon and sp3 hybridized carbon with dis-orderness ratio of 0.7. The prepared QDs exhibited pH dependent emission which might be useful for environment conservation. CQDs have zeta potential of 30 mV which suggested their stability and it can withstand for up to 30 days without forming aggregates in an open environment. The QDs were evaluated for their suitability for oil recovery. The S-dopped CQDs, were highly stable in aqueous as well as in brine solutions. It also increased the contact angle of oil on lime stone from 32.79° to 49.78° and increased the wettability of limestone along with modulating the capillary pressure thus increased the oil extraction from limestone by 17% (Baragau et al., 2021a). Wu et al. also confirmed that change in pressure can increase the recovery of oil from the extraction site. Petroleum coke was used as a precursor to producehydrophilicCDs (hf-CDs). The prepared CDs of 2.54 ± 0.016 nm were enriched with hydrophilic functional groups at the surface. It was recorded that the addition of 0.20 wt% hf-CDs reduces pressure by 23.81% and improved the oil recovery by 26.38% without using any surfactant (Wu et al., 2021).

Environmental pollution is also a major concern around the world (Ahuja, 2021; Ahuja and Bhatt, 2018; Khan et al., 2021; Vinayak et al., 2021). A variety of pollutants such as metals, antibiotics, pesticides, dyes, plastics are released into the environment and toxic to microflora, animals, and humans (Bhatia et al., 2021d; Choi et al., 2020; Gurav et al., 2020; Song et al., 2021). Miniature size along with the energy modulation capability of QDs offer some interesting opportunities that can be exploited at the commercial level for various purposes like detection of pollutants/target compounds, development of biosensors, and waste degradation. In environmental remediation, QDs can be used for the detection of contaminants as well as for waste degradation. The applications of QDs are summarized below:

Detection and biosensor: Photoluminescence and light-emitting potential of QDs can be utilized for the detection of pollutants and heavy metals. The main idea behind the detection is the affinity of QDs and changes in physical, physiological, or optical behaviour. The same potential can be used for the construction of biosensors. Most of the available reports emphasized on heavy metal detection. Wang and colleague (Wang et al., 2012) have used the CDs prepared from eggshell membrane for the detection of glutathione (GTH). QDs-ESM exhibited bluish fluorescence which is quenched in presence of Cu+2. The suppression of fluorescence depends upon the amount of copper ions. However, the addition of GTH restore the fluorescence as GTH has a high affinity for metal ions and bind to it thus neutralized the effect of metal ions on QDs fluorescence. As discussed in an earlier section, doping with heteroatoms can increase the possible applications due to the availability of binding sites. Bean pod and onion based QDs were dopped with sulphur and nitrogen. Prepared QDs, exhibiting blue emission can be used for Ag+ due to high affinity. In presence of Ag+, photoluminescence of QDs showed linear range within 0.1–25 μM. In addition, the concentration of Ag+ resulted in a colour change of S/N-CDs from yellow to brown under sunlight. The sensitivity of QDs can detect the presence of Ag+ in the water sample (Lu et al., 2019). Likewise, rice residues and glycine-based CDs offered a QY of 23.48%. CDs showed tremendous performance in the detection of Fe3+ and tetracycline with a linear range of 3.32–32.26 μM. The threshold concentration of chlortetracycline, tetracycline, and terramycin were 0.2791, 0.2367, and 0.3739 μM, respectively. Nitrogen dopped biomass based QDs were highly sensitive and selective for Fe3+ and antibiotic detection (Qi et al., 2019).

In another work, graphite CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment. Hydrothermal treatment increased oxygenated surface functionalization of GQDs (GQDs-M) and increased hydrophilicity of QDs. GQDs-M exhibited maximum emission against 340 nm excitation wavelength which was used for Fe+3 detection in a water sample. GQDs-M showed higher specificity towards Fe+3 detection in comparison to other metal ions including silver, cobalt, aluminum, chromium, copper, magnesium zinc, etc. Moreover, a linear relationship was observed between concentration and luminescence quenching between 1 and 50uM(Abbas et al., 2020). Fan and colleagues also suggested the possible use of food waste as raw material for CDs preparation. Thus, prepared CDs from food waste can be used for the detection of food pathogens and heavy metal contamination. However, the utilization and purpose rely on the origin of waste i.e. plant or animal (Fan et al., 2020). Glucose-based QDs (Glc-QDs), prepared from commercial-grade glucose solution in supercritical water by hydrothermal flow synthesis. Without any surface modification, Glc-QDs exhibited high selectivity and sensitivity for chromium (VI) ion and iron (II) even at very low concentrations i.e. 1.83 ppm and 6.09 ppm respectively (Baragau et al., 2021b).

Degradation and removal: Entrapment of energy and photoluminescence is a well-known characteristic of QDs. The potential can be utilized for photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. A photocatalyst was developed with CQDs (extracted from waste biomass) and Bi2WO6. CQDs were prepared with peach blossoms by hydrothermal carbonization and conjugated with Bi2WO6. The developed conjugates were employed for photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline. The photocatalytic action of conjugates relies on the availability of energy, and electrons. Owns toquantum effect, CQDs acts as a reservoir for electrons, and centre for light harvesting as well as pollutants degradation. Among different dosage sizes of the conjugate, 5% BC-QDs@Bi2WO6 effectively degraded the target antibiotic within 120 min. Conjugate absorb infra-red light and released shorter wavelength radiation along with free radicals which induce the degradation. Further increase in the concentration of conjugates, reduced the photocatalytic degradation as CQDs act as a recombinant centre rather than light-harvesting centers (Tang et al., 2019). In textile and paper industries, the use of dyes and stains are common out of which a significant fraction is discharged in effluent and deteriorate the water resources. Treatment of effluent and removal of chemical moieties are responsible for the economic burden on industries. TiO2 is one of the major photocatalysts used to degrade the pollutants however its efficiency against different organic pollutants is a matter of consideration. TiO2 has a lone pair of electrons which catalyzes the degradation of organic wastes but the availability of sufficient electrons, and charge separation is needed for efficient degradation. QDs act as reservoirs of electrons and energy that in a combination of photocatalysts improve photodegradation. Heng et al. prepared a composite of CQDs, TiO2, and N-doped CQDs, prepared from an oil palm frond. FT-IR and EDX spectrum of composite revealed the attachment of CQDs on TiO2 surface. Among different combinations of QDs and photocatalyst, NCQDs/TiO2-1 improved the MB degradation efficiency to 40.9% within 60 min in comparison to pure TiO2, offeringonly15.6% (Heng et al., 2020). A photocatalytic composite was prepared with CQDs of coconut shell and sycamore leaves and dopped with nitrogen by using urea. FeCl3·6H2O and H2BDC were used to prepare photocatalyst i.e., MIL-52(Fe). The composite was evaluated for the treatment of sewage samples containing chromium (VI) and MB as major pollutants. The presence of hydroxyl and carbonyl functional groups in QDs facilitated the interaction between catalyst and organic pollutants. This resulted in photocatalytic efficiencies of 69.31% and 90.13% for MB and Cr(VI) respectively (Qiang et al., 2021). Other researchers including Xie et al. (2021)and Si et al. (2021) also emphasized on the use of composite instead of bare CQDs from any origin for waste degradation. Xie and colleagues used a composite of CQDs, prepared with glucose and PEI as a precursor with Co(NO)3 for the degradation of chlorophenols. Catalyst composite exhibited removal efficiency for total organic carbon was around 99.7% with PMS contributed by both adsorptions as well as degradation. The TOC removal efficiency was reduced to 40.5% and 26.5% in the second and third cycles (Xie et al., 2020). Si et al. used conjugates of CQDs, prepared from straw and BiOCl for the degradation of bis-phenol A (Si et al., 2021).

QDs are the newest and smallest member of nano-structure family. The current QDs market size is about 4.0 billion USD and is expected to increase up to 8.6 billion USD by 2026 (marketsandmarkets.com/Market-Reports/quantum-dots-qd-market-694. html). Commercially QDs are mostly used in display devices as these offer higher brightness, contrast ratio, and better resolution with higher energy efficiency compared to conventional displays. Top key players in QDs market include Samsung Electronics (South Korea), Nonosys (US), NanocoGroup (UK), NNCrystals US Corporation (US), QD laser (Japan), Merck Group (Germany), NanoPhotonica (US), LG Electronics (South Korea) and DuPont (US). Most of the commercially available QDs are prepared using rare earth materials such as cadmium, selenium, zinc, and indium, etc. and these materials are very expensive due to low availability and higher demands. Due to environmental compatibility, non-toxicity, and renewable nature, CQDs from biomass and degradable waste materials have gained higher demand in the market. In comparison to semiconductor and metals-based QDs, the synthesis from biomass can be done under mild conditions. In spite of all these advantages still, very few companies are involved in CQDs production on a commercial scale. American Elements company has started CQDs production with a tunable emission fluorescence in the 450–550 nm range and have applications in solar cells, bioimaging, and as photocatalysts (https://www.americanelements.com/carbon-quantum-dots-7440-44-0). One another company, Suzhou Xingshuo Nanotech also produces CQDs of size 10 nm and their surface contains oxygen-containing hetero atom organic molecular light-emitting groups for use in display devices, tracer, biomedicine, and catalysts. (https://www.mesolight.com/companyxprofile.html). In terms of energy and waste management, there are still some challenges summarized below that remained to address in order to make biomass-QDs a complete alternative for non-renewable QDs.
i.
Uncontrolled particle size: Random size distribution and uncontrolled particle growth are some of the major drawbacks of most of the QDs synthesis routes like pyrolysis, hydrothermal, solvothermal or microwave assisted carbonization, etc. Even minor changes in particle size can change the chemical and optical properties enormously. Due to the wider particle range, additional efforts are needed for size-based separation to assist in precise applications.
ii.
Pre-availability of functional groups and heteroatoms: As biomass is composed of biomolecules that are rich in carbon with a small fraction of sulphur along with nitrogen, therefore, CQDs prepared from crude biomass are usually rich in carbonyl, and hydroxyl functional groups. In addition, nitrogen and sulphur may be available depending upon biomass composition and the method used for preparation. These functional groups are usually contributing to hydrophilicity of CDs and made CDs dispersible in aqueous and polar solvents but on the other hand, these functional groups hinder the interaction with non-polar compounds and oil. In oil recovery from oil spillage, hydrophobicity plays a crucial role as mentioned in an earlier section thus these functional groups must be modified. The modification reaction may affect the basic nature and behaviour of CDs. Similarly, the presence of additional heteroatomsmight be beneficial for most of the applications but in some cases, the presence may affect the interaction and response. Like in the case of detection kits and biosensors, the presence of heteroatoms may have a negative on the interaction between CDs and target analytes.
iii.
Stability and reusability: Stability, compatibility, and higher QY are also some obstacles to overcome. Most of the applications like waste detection, and degradation depend upon its affinity and type of obtained response. Very few reports are available for high QY in biomass-QDs and for a reliable detection system, the signal must be strong enough. Their further efforts are needed to work on that part as biosensors and photo-detection assisted applications to get a clear and distinct optical signal with higher QY. Waste, sewage, xenobiotics, etc., are some terms that itself define the harshness of the environment thus treatment system must be stable to act. CDs are prepared from biomass and mainly composed of carbon core which may be prone to a typical waste system having high temperature, pH, salinity, etc. In such an environment, CDs or their composite must be stable and recoverable for reuse. As mentioned in previous examples, some of the CDs and composites loses its efficacy of waste treatment by more than 40% in such case the designed system becomes ineffective after 2–3 cycle and just need to add the catalyst again which may have a high cost.
iv.
Scalability and replicability: Most of the available data and reports showed the lab experimental results of with simulated system but in the natural environment, the behaviour may be different. Therefore, the exact result may be different from the simulation especially in the case of oil recovery and extraction, waste degradation as natural microflora and trace elements may also influence (positively or negatively) the interaction.
v.
Leakage and heavy metal pollution:In environmental conservation and energy application use of CQDs composites with transition and heavy metals. Usually, CDs are degradable thus after completing lifespan the traces of metals are left in the system. Even during the active period, there may be a case of leakage of metals from the composites. In both cases, the composite pollutes the system which should be avoided thus complete recovery must be ensured after degradation or completion of the cycle.

However, the obstacles can't hinder the growth of CDs in different sectors, and it is possible that in upcoming years these obstacles remained history and we will be able to create a green and safe environment with sustainable growth.

QDs are a new class of nanomaterial that are also defined as zero-dimension nanoparticles. In the current time, the focus has been shifted from semiconductors and metals to natural, renewable, and degradable substrates. In the same line, waste biomass, vegetable, and fruit peel, and agricultural biomass can be used as a precursor for QDs synthesis. The ease in tunability in QDs physical, chemical, and optical properties make them fit for application. CQDs act as a harvester for electrons and energy and thus improve the conversion of solar energy to electricity and are used in the construction of solar cells. Tremendous optical behaviour and stable photoluminescence allow its use in the fabrication of biosensors for the detection of pollutants and chemicals along with photocatalytic degradation of waste. The contribution of QDs and nanomaterial in various industries open an era of endless opportunity. However, it is still necessary to construct a stable waste treatment system for wastewater and xenobiotics at a large scale.
